narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings—pouring your heart into law school dreams only to have the door close can feel like a punch to the gut, and I'm truly sorry you're facing this right now. The admissions model looked at your profile and saw a 44.7% chance of not passing the bar exam, which tipped the scales against your application, but let me walk you through the story it pieced together from your details, like chapters in a book that didn't quite align for success. The biggest chapter was your Year 1 grades, landing you in the 1st decile at school—that early struggle stood out as the strongest signal pulling toward potential bar challenges, almost like starting a marathon with a heavy weight on your back. Then came your Year 3 decile, also a 1, reinforcing that pattern and whispering doubts about building momentum over time. Your LSAT score of 34 added to the weight, signaling to the model that the raw analytical edge it expects wasn't quite there to overcome those hurdles. Even your middle family income played a role, hinting at fewer resources for the intense prep that often makes the difference. Your Asian background factored in too, as the model noted patterns where similar profiles face steeper odds. Your undergrad GPA of 3.1, full-time student status, and being female each nudged the prediction toward failure in their own subtle ways, weaving a picture of someone the model fears might not cross the bar finish line. It's not that you're incapable—far from it—but this specific blend painted a cautious tale for them. The good news? You're in control of rewriting your story: targeted prep could lift those deciles next time, and many have turned similar profiles around with grit and strategy. You've got the drive—target_law says it all—and this is just one plot twist, not the ending. Keep pushing; brighter chapters await.
